facts_disclosed: Parents are from Guadalajara, family moved to the US when Elena was 4

turns.content: random question but do you know anything about making tamales because I'm about to attempt something possibly insane
Ha, I love this energy! I know the basics — tamales are a labor of love. What's the occasion, and how insane are we talking? Like "first time ever" insane or "trying a complicated variation" insane?

first time ever insane lol. so my mom has always made tamales from scratch, it's her recipe from when she grew up in Guadalajara and they are genuinely the best thing I've ever eaten. I've helped her before but I've never tried to make them on my own and I want to surprise her

That's such a sweet idea, she's going to love that. Tamales from a Guadalajara family recipe are the real deal. So when you've helped her before, which parts did you do? That'll help me figure out where you might need the most guidance. The masa, the filling, the spreading, the wrapping?

mostly the wrapping and steaming part, she always handled the masa and the filling herself. she's very particular about her masa she says the secret is the lard ratio and she always does it by feel which is NOT helpful when you're trying to learn lmao

The "by feel" cooks are the most skilled and the most impossible to learn from! Classic mom move. OK so for the masa, a good starting point is about a 1:1 ratio of masa harina to broth by volume, and then for the lard, you want roughly 2/3 cup of lard per 2 cups of masa harina. Beat the lard until it's fluffy first, then gradually mix in the masa and warm broth. You'll know the consistency is right when a small ball of masa floats in water. What filling is she known for — pork, chicken, cheese and chile?

pork in red chile sauce, that's the signature. and also she does a sweet one with pineapple and raisins that my sister goes crazy for. I was going to just try the pork ones for now because I don't want to overwhelm myself. the float test is smart I've seen her do that actually

Smart call sticking with just the pork. For the red chile sauce, the key is using dried chiles — guajillo and ancho are the classic combo for a Guadalajara-style red sauce. Toast them lightly, soak in hot water for 20-30 minutes, then blend with garlic, cumin, a little oregano, and some of the soaking liquid. For the pork, you can braise a pork shoulder low and slow, then shred it and mix it with the sauce. How many are you trying to make?

ok so this is where it gets ambitious... I want to make enough for my whole family so probably like 3-4 dozen?? my parents, my sister, my tia Rosa who always comes over. we moved here from Guadalajara when I was 4 and tamales have always been like the thing that connects us to home you know, it's never just food it's the whole tradition

I love that — tamales really are one of those foods that carry so much more than flavor, they carry memory and identity. Three to four dozen is totally doable for one person, but give yourself a full day. Seriously, don't try to rush it. Start the pork the night before or early morning so it has time to braise and cool. Then do your masa and assembly. The biggest mistake first-timers make is spreading the masa too thick on the husk — aim for about 1/4 inch. When is the big reveal?

I'm thinking next weekend, I have Saturday and Sunday off which literally never happens. and yeah I'm going to need the whole day probably because knowing me I'll mess up the first 10 and then get the hang of it. my mom is going to either cry or tell me I did it wrong or both lol

Probably both at the same time, that's the most mom response possible! A couple more tips: soak your corn husks in warm water for at least an hour so they're pliable, and don't overfill — that's the other big beginner mistake. When you wrap, fold the bottom up and leave the top open, or tie them if your family does it that way. And steam them standing up with the open end on top for about 60-90 minutes. You'll know they're done when the masa pulls away from the husk cleanly.

ok writing all of this down. the standing up thing I definitely knew because I remember being little and watching my mom and my abuela line them all up in the pot like little soldiers. dios mio I miss my abuela she would have loved this

That image of them lined up like little soldiers is so sweet. And I bet your abuela would be incredibly proud that you're carrying this forward. That's exactly what food traditions are about — they pass through hands and generations. Your mom learned from your abuela, and now you're learning too, even if your teacher is a mix of memory and an AI chatbot instead of standing in the kitchen together.

ok you're going to make me cry and I'm at work right now lol. but you're right that's exactly what it is. I think that's why I want to do this, not just to surprise my mom but to prove to myself I can keep this going. our family traditions are everything to me especially being so far from Mexico

That's a beautiful reason. And honestly, even if they come out a little different from your mom's, the fact that you made them with that intention is what matters. She'll taste the love in them even if the masa is a little thick. Do you want me to put together a simple timeline for your Saturday so you can plan out the whole day step by step?
yes please that would be amazing, I'm a planner and having a schedule will keep me from panicking when the masa doesn't float or whatever lol

Here's your Saturday tamale timeline:

**Friday night:** Season and start braising the pork shoulder (or do it Saturday at 7am if you want it fresh). Soak dried chiles.

**8-9am:** Make the red chile sauce. Shred the pork and mix with sauce. Soak corn husks in warm water.

**9-10:30am:** Make the masa. Beat lard until fluffy, gradually add masa harina and warm broth. Do the float test.

**10:30am-1pm:** Assembly line — spread masa, add filling, wrap. First few will be ugly, that's normal.

**1-2:30pm:** Steam the tamales, 60-90 minutes.

**3pm:** Show up at your mom's house with tamales and watch her reaction.

That gives you buffer time for mistakes. You've got this!

this is perfect I just screenshotted it. ok I'm actually excited now instead of terrified. thank you so much, I'll let you know how it goes if I remember. my mom is either going to be so proud or roast me mercilessly and honestly both outcomes are great

Either way it's going to be a great day! Remember, the float test is your friend, don't spread the masa too thick, and give yourself grace on the first batch. Your family is lucky to have someone who cares this much about keeping traditions alive. Good luck next Saturday — I'm rooting for you and the tamales!
